section.title: Personal Greetings
section.sectionRationale: Having finished the great argument of the letter, Paul closes with a long roll of names. He commends Phoebe, a servant of the church at Cenchrea who carries the letter to Rome; he greets Prisca and Aquila, who risked their necks for him, and the church that meets in their house; then name after name, men and women, Jews and Gentiles, coworkers, kinsmen, and the beloved. It looks at first like a list to be skimmed, but it is nothing less than the gospel made visible as a family.
section.thesis: The gospel does not create an idea or an institution only; it creates a real, diverse, laboring family, and the risen Christ knows and honors each of its ordinary members by name.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Phoebe commended as a trusted servant of the church; Prisca and Aquila and the household churches honored for their risk and labor; and the long roll of named saints, women and men, who together make up the beloved family of God in Rome.

units.label: Phoebe, our sister and servant of the church
units.note: a trusted saint commended and carrying the letter

verses.text: I commend to you our sister Phoebe, a {{diakonos|servant}} of the church in Cenchrea.
Welcome her in the Lord in a manner worthy of the saints, and assist her with anything she may need from you. For she has been a great help to many people, including me.

units.label: Prisca and Aquila and the church in their house
units.note: fellow workers who risked their lives

verses.text: Greet Prisca and Aquila, my {{sunergos|fellow workers}} in Christ Jesus,
who have risked their lives for me. Not only I but all the churches of the Gentiles are grateful to them.
Greet also the church that meets at their house. Greet my {{agapetos|beloved}} Epenetus, who was the first convert to Christ in the province of Asia.

units.label: The roll of the beloved saints
units.note: women and men, Jews and Gentiles, named one by one

verses.text: Greet Mary, who has worked very hard for you.
Greet Andronicus and Junia, my fellow countrymen and fellow prisoners. They are outstanding among the {{apostolos|apostles}}, and they were in Christ before I was.
Greet Ampliatus, my beloved in the Lord.
Greet Urbanus, our fellow worker in Christ, and my beloved Stachys.
Greet Apelles, who is approved in Christ. Greet those who belong to the household of Aristobulus.
Greet Herodion, my fellow countryman. Greet those from the household of Narcissus who are in the Lord.
Greet Tryphena and Tryphosa, women who have worked hard in the Lord. Greet my beloved Persis, who has worked very hard in the Lord.
Greet Rufus, chosen in the Lord, and his mother, who has been a mother to me as well.
Greet Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermes, Patrobas, Hermas, and the brothers with them.
Greet Philologus and Julia, Nereus and his sister, and Olympas and all the saints with them.
{{aspazomai|Greet}} one another with a holy kiss. All the churches of Christ send you greetings.

diakonos.gloss: A servant, minister, or attendant; the word from which we get deacon. Paul applies it to Phoebe as a servant of the church at Cenchrea. It describes one who serves the body of Christ in real, practical ministry, whether understood as a general term of honored service or as the recognized office of deaconess.

sunergos.gloss: A fellow worker, one who labors alongside another in a shared task. Paul uses it of Prisca and Aquila, coworkers in Christ Jesus who toiled and risked their lives for the gospel. The gospel enlists a team of laborers, not spectators, joined together in one work.

agapetos.gloss: Beloved, dearly loved. Paul sprinkles the word through the list, calling Epenetus, Ampliatus, Stachys, and Persis his beloved. It is the same word the Father speaks over the Son, now overflowing onto ordinary saints who are dear to God and to one another in Christ.

apostolos.gloss: One sent with a commission; an apostle or messenger. Paul says Andronicus and Junia are outstanding among the apostles, which may mean they were esteemed by the apostles or were themselves notable messengers sent by the churches. Either way it honors their labor and standing in Christ.

aspazomai.gloss: To greet, welcome, or embrace warmly. The word drums through the chapter as Paul greets saint after saint and calls them to greet one another with a holy kiss. It is the affectionate language of family, the warm recognition of those bound together in the Lord.

basic.context: Paul has come to the end of his letter and now sends greetings, as ancient letters commonly did. But this list is unusually long and warm. He first commends Phoebe, almost certainly the one carrying the letter to Rome, then greets some twenty-six people by name plus several households and unnamed brothers and sisters. He has never visited Rome, yet he knows and loves many there. The list is a window into a real congregation.
basic.hermeneutics: Resist the temptation to skim the names. Read the list as a portrait of the church the gospel creates: diverse in sex, race, and status, bound by love and labor. Notice how many are women, how many are called beloved, how many are praised for hard work, and how many bear Greek, Latin, and Jewish names. The details are the point: the gospel gathers particular people into one family.
basic.originalLanguages: Phoebe is a diakonos, a servant of the church at Cenchrea. Prisca and Aquila are Paul's sunergos, fellow workers who labored beside him. Again and again a saint is agapetos, beloved. Andronicus and Junia are noted among the apostolos, the sent ones. And the whole passage rings with aspazomai, the warm word to greet, as Paul calls this family to embrace one another with a holy kiss.
basic.typology: The roll of the redeemed recalls the way Scripture loves to name God's people, from the tribes of Israel to the register of those who returned from exile. As God once numbered His people, so He knows His new-covenant family by name. The many nations gathered here answer the promise that in Abraham's seed all the families of the earth would be blessed.
basic.christ: Every name in the list is defined by Christ: they are in Christ Jesus, in the Lord, chosen in the Lord, approved in Christ, the first convert to Christ. Christ is the center that draws this diverse company into one. The churches that send greetings are the churches of Christ. He is the Lord in whom they are welcomed, and His love is the bond that makes strangers into a family.
basic.god: God is the one who has been building this family throughout the letter, and here we see the result: a people loved by God, chosen in the Lord, gathered from Jew and Gentile, high and low. The God who justifies the ungodly has knit these particular sinners into a household of faith, and He honors their ordinary labors and love.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 18:1-3 and 18:18-26 record Prisca and Aquila laboring with Paul and instructing Apollos. 1 Corinthians 16:19 shows a church meeting in their house. Philippians 4:3 names women who labored side by side with Paul in the gospel. Romans 12:10 had already urged the believers to love one another with brotherly affection, which this list embodies.
basic.oneStory: The gospel Paul has expounded for fifteen chapters lands in a living room in Rome, in a company of named people who love and labor together. The story that ran from Abraham to Christ now gathers Jews and Gentiles, women and men, into one beloved family. This is what God was aiming at all along: not merely saved individuals but a reconciled people who greet one another with a holy kiss.

context.literary: This is the first half of the letter's extended closing, a section of greetings framed by the commendation of Phoebe at the start and the exchange of greetings between churches at the end. The repeated verb greet gives it a steady rhythm. It belongs with the final warnings and doxology that follow, together rounding off the whole epistle.
context.historical: Paul writes around AD 57, probably from Corinth, and Cenchrea was Corinth's eastern port, which fits Phoebe carrying the letter. Many named here bear names common among slaves and freedmen, and some belong to great households like those of Aristobulus and Narcissus. The list reflects a real cross section of the imperial capital: immigrants, tradespeople, and servants, Jews and Gentiles together.
context.canonical: The chapter shows the doctrine of the letter embodied in a concrete congregation. Justification by faith and life in the Spirit produce a reconciled community. The prominence of women who serve and labor here stands alongside the wider New Testament witness and shows the honored place of faithful women in the earliest churches.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To commend Phoebe to the Roman church so they would receive and support her, and to greet by name the many believers he knew in Rome, strengthening his bond with a church he had not yet visited and honoring the labor and love of ordinary saints.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the particular members of one first-century congregation. Its abiding lesson is prescriptive by pattern rather than by command: the gospel forms a diverse, affectionate, laboring family in which each member is known and valued, and such love and honor should mark the church in every age.

debates.question: In what sense is Phoebe a diakonos of the church at Cenchrea?

views.label: A recognized servant or deaconess
views.case: Paul attaches the term to a specific local church, which many take to indicate a recognized ministry of service, so that Phoebe is honored as a deaconess who cared for the practical needs of the saints.

views.label: A general term of honored service
views.case: Others read diakonos here in its broad sense of one who serves, praising Phoebe's tireless help to many, including Paul, without pressing it into a fixed office.

debates.bearing: On either reading Phoebe is a trusted, honored servant of Christ's church whom Paul asks the Romans to welcome and assist; the passage clearly commends faithful female service in the body of Christ.

debates.question: What does it mean that Andronicus and Junia are outstanding among the apostles?

views.label: Well known to the apostles
views.case: The phrase can mean they were highly regarded by the apostles, esteemed by the recognized circle of Christ's sent ones without themselves holding that office.

views.label: Notable messengers among a wider group of sent ones
views.case: Others take it to mean they were prominent among a broader company of messengers commissioned by the churches, laborers in the mission, while the unique foundational office of the Twelve and Paul remains distinct.

debates.bearing: Read fairly, the verse honors Andronicus and Junia as esteemed, early, and hardworking believers, without overturning the historic distinction of the foundational apostolic office; it plainly dignifies their gospel labor.

study.originalLanguages: The passage is stitched together with a few weighty words. Phoebe is a diakonos, a servant of her church, worthy of a welcome and help. Prisca and Aquila are sunergos, fellow workers who spent themselves and even risked death for the gospel. Over and over a saint is agapetos, beloved, the very word of the Father's delight now resting on ordinary Christians. Andronicus and Junia stand out among the apostolos, the sent ones. And the whole roll pulses with aspazomai, the warm command to greet and embrace one another in the Lord.

typology.fulfillment: As Scripture numbers the tribes and registers those who return from exile, so Christ knows His new-covenant family by name, gathered here in Rome.

typology.type: The blessing of the nations in Abraham
typology.fulfillment: The Jews and Gentiles named side by side fulfill the promise that in Abraham's seed all the families of the earth would be blessed.

typology.type: The holy kiss of the family of God
typology.fulfillment: The holy kiss enacts the reconciliation the gospel accomplishes, making former strangers into one embracing household in Christ.

study.christ: Though this is a list of human names, Christ is on every line. These believers are in Christ Jesus, in the Lord, chosen in the Lord, approved in Christ, the first fruits for Christ. He is the gravitational center that pulls slaves and nobles, Jews and Greeks, women and men into one body. The churches that greet them are the churches of Christ. The whole passage quietly proclaims that union with Christ is what makes a people out of strangers, and that He knows and treasures each one.
study.god: The God who has been the subject of this letter, justifying the ungodly and pouring out His Spirit, is here seen gathering a family. He loves these people, He has chosen them, He has set them to their labors and honored their love. The passage displays a God who does not save in the abstract but binds real, named sinners into a household, delighting to call them beloved.

crossReferences.note: Paul first meets Aquila and Priscilla in Corinth and works with them as tentmakers, the fellow workers he now greets.
Priscilla and Aquila take Apollos aside and explain the way of God more accurately, showing their gift for teaching.
Paul names women who labored side by side with him in the gospel, as Mary, Tryphena, Tryphosa, and Persis do here.
The command to love one another with genuine, brotherly affection is embodied in this warm roll of greetings.

study.oneStory: The long argument of Romans comes to rest in a room full of people. The gospel that reconciles sinners to God also reconciles them to one another, so that Jew and Gentile, master and slave, woman and man are gathered as one beloved family. From the promise to Abraham to this house church in Rome, God has been assembling a people for His Son. The list of names is the harvest of the gospel, ordinary saints known and loved by the risen Lord.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe Phoebe as he commends her to the Roman church?

options.text: As our sister and a servant of the church in Cenchrea, to be welcomed and helped.
options.note: Paul calls her a sister and a diakonos of her church, worthy of a welcome and assistance.

options.text: As a wealthy patron who needs no help from the Romans.
options.note: She was indeed a benefactor of many, yet Paul still asks the Romans to assist her, not to leave her unaided.

options.text: As a stranger Paul barely knows.
options.note: Paul warmly commends her as a sister and a great help to many, including himself.

options.text: As someone the church should treat with suspicion.
options.note: Paul urges a welcome worthy of the saints, not suspicion.

quiz.q: What did Prisca and Aquila do that Paul highlights?

options.text: They were his fellow workers who risked their own lives for him.
options.note: Paul honors them as coworkers who risked their necks for him, thanked by all the Gentile churches.

options.text: They gave money but avoided any personal risk.
options.note: They did help materially, but Paul specifically says they risked their lives, not merely their resources.

options.text: They founded the church in Rome by themselves.
options.note: They were genuine coworkers, but Paul does not credit them with founding the Roman church.

options.text: They opposed Paul's mission to the Gentiles.
options.note: Far from opposing it, all the churches of the Gentiles are grateful to them.

quiz.q: What does the sheer variety of names in this list reveal about the church?

options.text: The gospel gathers a diverse family of women and men, Jews and Gentiles, high and low, into one body.
options.note: The mix of names and backgrounds shows the reconciling reach of the gospel.

options.text: The church was made up only of prominent, high-status Romans.
options.note: Some named here were of high standing, but many names belong to slaves and freedmen, so it was not only the prominent.

options.text: The church excluded women from meaningful service.
options.note: Many women are named and praised for their hard work in the Lord.

options.text: The church was uniformly Jewish.
options.note: Jewish kinsmen are named, but so are many Gentiles; the church was mixed.

quiz.q: How does Paul repeatedly describe individual believers in the list?

options.text: As beloved, and as those who have worked hard in the Lord.
options.note: Paul calls several beloved and praises many for their labor in the Lord.

options.text: As useless members to be tolerated.
options.note: He honors them as beloved and hardworking, never as useless.

options.text: As employees owed wages for their service.
options.note: Their service is real labor, but it is in the Lord and out of love, not a matter of wages.

options.text: As anonymous faces not worth mentioning.
options.note: The whole point is that Paul names them one by one and honors them.

quiz.q: How are the believers told to greet one another, and who sends greetings to them?

options.text: With a holy kiss, and all the churches of Christ send them greetings.
options.note: Paul calls for the holy kiss of the family and conveys greetings from all the churches of Christ.

options.text: With a formal handshake, and only Paul greets them.
options.note: The text says a holy kiss, and greetings come from all the churches, not Paul alone.

options.text: With cool politeness befitting strangers.
options.note: The holy kiss is the warm affection of family, not cool politeness.

options.text: With greetings only from the church in Jerusalem.
options.note: Greetings do come from other churches, but Paul says all the churches of Christ send them, not Jerusalem alone.

questions: Paul commends Phoebe and asks the church to welcome and help her. How well does your church honor and support those who quietly serve its practical needs?
Prisca and Aquila risked their lives for the gospel and for Paul. What would it look like for you to take a real risk for the sake of a brother or sister in Christ?
Paul knows and names some twenty-six people, calling many of them beloved. How does it change your view of the church to see it as a family of particular, known, and loved people rather than a crowd?
So many in this list are praised for hard work in the Lord. Where is God calling you to labor for His people, even when the work is unseen?
The believers are told to greet one another with a holy kiss and are joined to all the churches of Christ. How can you show warmer, more concrete affection to the wider family of God?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered servant is diakonos, from which we get deacon; a footnote in some editions offers deaconess. It may denote a recognized ministry of service or the general sense of one who serves; either way it honors Phoebe's faithful work for her church.
Prisca is the shorter form of Priscilla; see Acts 18:2. Paul uses Prisca in his letters, Luke tends to use Priscilla. The same beloved coworker is meant.

The name Junia is most likely a woman's name; a few take it as the masculine Junias. The phrase outstanding among the apostles can mean well known to the apostles or notable among a wider group of sent ones; both honor their standing in Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 16:1-16. Footnote markers were omitted and line breaks rendered as single spaces per the spine rules. No dashes appear in the text. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
